fab_section_md:
- He burned the poetic name of the war into history (fab-the-blacks-and-the-greens-16-p01:17)
- Grand Maester Munkun wrote The Dance of the Dragons, A True Telling, the most detailed account, though composed a generation later and relying on Orwyle (fab-the-blacks-and-the-greens-16-p01:55)

- Septon Eustace, writing years later, held the king's death anticipated and the greens prepared, and gave a gentler account of Aegon (fab-the-blacks-and-the-greens-16-p01:39)
- In his telling Criston Cole cut Beesbury's throat (fab-the-blacks-and-the-greens-16-p01:99)
identity_line: Septon Eustace, writing years later, held the king's death anticipated and the greens prepared, and gave a gentler account of Aegon.

fab_section_md:
- He argued against burning King's Landing and would command the fleet closing the Gullet (fab-the-blacks-and-the-greens-16-p01:225)
- Corlys Velaryon of Driftmark, the Sea Snake, grown old, was the greatest lord to pledge his strength to Rhaenyra (fab-the-blacks-and-the-greens-16-p01:197)
- He grumbled but smiled that the three boys were Velaryons (fab-the-blacks-and-the-greens-16-p01:261)
- Lady Jeyne, the Maiden of the Vale, ruled the Eyrie, and her own rights might be questioned if Rhaenyra were set aside (fab-the-blacks-and-the-greens-16-p01:149)
identity_line: Lady Jeyne, the Maiden of the Vale, ruled the Eyrie, and her own rights might be questioned if Rhaenyra were set aside.

fab_section_md:
- Grand Maester Orwyle sat on the green council and opened it by reviewing the customary rites for a dead king (fab-the-blacks-and-the-greens-16-p01:61)
- His confessions before execution underlie Munkun's history, though he wrote to absolve himself (fab-the-blacks-and-the-greens-16-p01:55)
